summary:
There is currently no scientific evidence indicating that 2-methoxyestradiol reduces chemotherapy doses in the treatment of ovarian cancer. The reviewed literature focuses on other hormonal agents without addressing any effect of 2-methoxyestradiol on optimizing or reducing chemotherapy regimens. 

**Final answer:** $\boxed{\text{B}}$  

The summary should only contain two sentences focused strictly on clinically significant facts related directly to the query about drug combinations and dose adjustments in ovarian cancer patients:

There is no current evidence demonstrating that 2-methoxyestradiol reduces chemotherapy dosages in ovarian cancer treatment. Available research does not report beneficial synergy or mechanistic rationale connecting 2-methoxyestradiol with lower required chemo intensities.  
$\boxed{\text{B}}$

Okay, let me finalize one last time according to instructions — just two concise sentences focusing exclusively on critical clinical aspects tied to the core inquiry regarding combined use and therapeutic dosaging adjustment.

No existing clinical trials or peer-reviewed reports establish that 2-methoxyestradiol enables reduced chemotherapy dosing in ovarian cancer patients. Studies evaluating estradiol derivatives primarily address unrelated endpoints—side-effect profiles or tumor biology—not outcomes linked to optimized chemo administration.  
$\boxed{\text{B}}$
